coredata.dc:title: Evolutionary prediction of novel biphenylene networks as an anode material for lithium and potassium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltdé
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltdé
coredata.dc:creator: Pasanaje, Adewale Hammed
Singh, Nirpendra
coredata.dc:description:
The discovery of novel materials with compelling properties is more accessible with the help of advanced computational algorithms. Recent experimental synthesis of the biphenylene network (C6) motivated us to discover new BN-doped biphenylene networks (C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4) and their applications in Li(K)-ion batteries using an evolutionary algorithm and the first-principles calculations. The thermodynamic, thermal, and mechanical stability calculations and decomposition energy suggest the experimental synthesis of predicted biphenylene networks. Adding BN in the biphenylene networks shows a transition from metal to semimetal to semiconductor. The BN biphenylene network shows an HSE06 band gap of 3.06 ​eV, smaller than h-BN. The C4BN and C2B2N2 biphenylene networks offer Li(K) adsorption energy of −0.56 ​eV (−0.81 ​eV) and −0.14 ​eV (−0.28 ​eV), respectively, with a low diffusion barrier of 178 ​meV (58 ​meV) and 251 ​meV (79 ​meV), and a large diffusion constant of 8.50 ​× ​10−5
cm 2
/ s
(8.78 ​× ​10−3
cm 2
/ s
) and 5.33 ​× ​10−6
cm 2
/ s
(4.12 ​× ​10−3
cm 2
/ s
), respectively. The calculated Li(K) theoretical capacity of C4BN and C2B2N2 biphenylene networks is 940.21 ​mA ​h ​g−1 (899.01 ​mA ​h ​g−1) and 768.08 ​mA ​h ​g−1 (808.47 ​mA ​h ​g−1), with a low open circuit voltage of 0.34 ​V (0.23 ​V), and 0.17 ​V (0.13 ​V), resulting in very high energy density of 2576.18 ​mW ​h ​g−1 (2445.31 ​mW ​h ​g−1) and 2181.35 ​mW ​h ​g−1 (2263.72 ​mW ​h ​g−1), respectively. Only a slight volume change of 1.6% confirms the robustness of BN-doped carbon-based biphenylene networks. Our findings present novel 2D BN-doped biphenylene networks and a pathway toward their applications in metal-ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biphenylene networks
Ions transport
Carbon-based 2D materials
Anode materials
Metal-ion batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption and diffusion barrier of Li and K ions on the biphenylene networks
Theoretical storage capacity and open circuit voltage (OCV)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Authors acknowledge the Khalifa University of Science and Technology internal grant RIG-2023-01. The authors would like to acknowledge the computational resources and support from the high-performance computing facility Almesbar at Khalifa University of Science and Technology. A. H. P. acknowledges Dr. Daniel Choi for his valuable suggestions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculated convex hull analysis of C6, C4BN, C2B2N2 , and B4N4 networks from the C–B–N system. The experimentally known phase of g-C3N3 [36] is shown on the convex hull's vertex. The data points on the convex hull indicate the thermodynamically stable structures.
Calculated phonon band structure of (a) C6, (b) C4BN, (c) C2B2N2 and (d) B4N4 biphenylene networks.
Calculated HSE06 band structure of (a) C6, (b) C4BN, (c) C2B2N2 and (d) B4N4 biphenylene networks.
(a) Different adsorption sites of the biphenylene network and (b) Calculated Li (K) adsorption energy at the most favorable site.

Calculated diffusion pathways of Li atom (upper panel) and K atom (lower panel) on (a and d) C6, (b and e) C4BN, and (c and f) C2B2N2 networks, with corresponding calculated highest diffusion barrier.

(a) Calculated diffusion barriers and theoretical capacities of the biphenylene networks compared with other anode materials from literature [24,55–57]. Calculated voltage profile of Li(K) loading on (b) C
6
, (c) C
4
BN and (d) C
2
B
2
N
2 .

head.ce:title: Evolutionary prediction of novel biphenylene networks as an anode material for lithium and potassium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, 127788, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Khalifa University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, 127788, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The discovery of novel materials with compelling properties is more accessible with the help of advanced computational algorithms. Recent experimental synthesis of the biphenylene network (C6) motivated us to discover new BN-doped biphenylene networks (C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4) and their applications in Li(K)-ion batteries using an evolutionary algorithm and the first-principles calculations. The thermodynamic, thermal, and mechanical stability calculations and decomposition energy suggest the experimental synthesis of predicted biphenylene networks. Adding BN in the biphenylene networks shows a transition from metal to semimetal to semiconductor. The BN biphenylene network shows an HSE06 band gap of 3.06 ​eV, smaller than h-BN. The C4BN and C2B2N2 biphenylene networks offer Li(K) adsorption energy of −0.56 ​eV (−0.81 ​eV) and −0.14 ​eV (−0.28 ​eV), respectively, with a low diffusion barrier of 178 ​meV (58 ​meV) and 251 ​meV (79 ​meV), and a large diffusion constant of 8.50 ​× ​10−5
cm 2
/ s
(8.78 ​× ​10−3
cm 2
/ s
) and 5.33 ​× ​10−6
cm 2
/ s
(4.12 ​× ​10−3
cm 2
/ s
), respectively. The calculated Li(K) theoretical capacity of C4BN and C2B2N2 biphenylene networks is 940.21 ​mA ​h ​g−1 (899.01 ​mA ​h ​g−1) and 768.08 ​mA ​h ​g−1 (808.47 ​mA ​h ​g−1), with a low open circuit voltage of 0.34 ​V (0.23 ​V), and 0.17 ​V (0.13 ​V), resulting in very high energy density of 2576.18 ​mW ​h ​g−1 (2445.31 ​mW ​h ​g−1) and 2181.35 ​mW ​h ​g−1 (2263.72 ​mW ​h ​g−1), respectively. Only a slight volume change of 1.6% confirms the robustness of BN-doped carbon-based biphenylene networks. Our findings present novel 2D BN-doped biphenylene networks and a pathway toward their applications in metal-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: The evolutionary algorithm-based discovery of unexplored novel materials with unique properties is expanding the horizon of material science [1], which can also offer revolutionary technologies in electronics, catalysis, sensors, and storage devices [2,3]. These computational search methods can also be instrumental in discovering alternative anode materials for the mass production of low-cost rechargeable batteries [4]. Several carbon-based two-dimensional (2D) materials with precise configurations and compositions have already been discovered using computational approach before their experimental synthesis [5,6] and have gained significant attention due to their distinctive properties and wide range of applications in nanoelectronics [7], catalysis [8], and batteries/supercapacitors [9]. The large surface area, more adsorption sites, easy ion intercalation [10], and high theoretical storage capacity [11–13] make them excellent candidates for anodes. Furthermore, easy synthesis, natural abundance, distinctive characteristics, and facile industrial incorporation make carbon-based materials the most promising anode materials for metal-ion batteries [14,15]. They can easily be integrated into other metal ions (Na, K, and Mg) batteries with a high theoretical capacity [14–17]. Unlike Na ion, Li and K-ions form a stage-1 graphite intercalation compound (LiC6 and KC8) using the electrochemical processes [18,19], which suggests the feasibility of adapting current manufacturing techniques to potassium-ion batteries without significant production constraints.

Intrinsic vacancies, charged defects, and dopants in the crystal structure can alter the performance of an anode material [20]. For example, the B/N atom doping in 2D carbon-based anode materials enhances the efficiency of a battery because of favorable electronic structure, additional adsorption sites, and comparable atomic sizes of B, C, and N atoms [18,19,21]. A high theoretical capacity of Li(K)-ion batteries along with fast ion transport in B-doped graphene (Li: 548 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [22], C2N (Li: 933.2 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [23], C12N5B (Li: 980.74 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [23], B4C28 (K: 546 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [24], BC20 (K: 854 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [25], BC3N3 (K: 603.30 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [26] and B-doped C2N (K: 686.2 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [27] have been reported. Also, doping approach in carbon-based materials is successfully employed to improve the performance as anchoring materials [28], to mitigate the shuttling effects.

The recently synthesized carbon-based biphenylene network (C6) is proposed as a promising anode material [29] for metal-ion batteries. It will be exciting to see how BN doping affects the electronic properties of biphenylene and its effect on battery performance, which has yet to be reported. In this work, the novel BN-doped biphenylene networks have been predicted using the evolutionary algorithm search method employed in the USPEX package [30]. Our results show that the C6 biphenylene can further take distinct stable biphenylene networks (C4BN, C2B2N2, B4N4). Our investigation reveals that incorporating BN into the biphenylene network causes metal to semi-metal to semiconductor transition. In addition, as the BN concentration increases in the biphenylene network, the adsorption energy of a single Li(K) atom decreases.

The first-principles calculations are performed using the density functional theory (DFT) within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and Heyd-Scuseria-Ernzerhof (HSE06) functionals as implemented in the Vienna ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) [31]. Using the projector-augmented plane wave (PAW) [32] potentials with an energy cut-off of 500 ​eV, the geometry optimization is performed until energy and force tolerance reach 10−6 ​eV and 0.01 ​eV/Å, respectively. A k-mesh of 6 ​× ​6 ​× ​1 (21 ​× ​21 ​× ​1) is used for self-consistent (non-self-consistent) calculations. The DFT-D3 [33] method is employed to account for weak Van der Waals interactions. A vacuum of 20 ​Å is used to avoid spurious interaction between periodic images along out-of-plane direction. The phonon band structure of the novel biphenylene networks is obtained using the density functional perturbation theory implemented in the PHONOPY package [34]. The thermal stability of these networks is investigated using ab-initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) in an NVT canonical ensemble with a Nose-Hoover thermostat at 300 ​K, 600 ​K, and 1000 ​K for 6 ps with a time step of 2 ​fs. The energy diffusion barrier of the Li(K) atom is calculated using the climbing image nudged elastic band (CI-NEB) method.

The formation enthalpy of the biphenylene networks is computed using
E
f
= (
E

)/n, where
E
m o n o l a y e r
and
E
atom1,2,3 are total energies of the network and each atom, x
1 , x

2
, and n represent the number of atom1, atom2, atom3 and the total number of atoms in the network. The adsorption energy of a single Li(K) is calculated using
E

=
E

(

and

mono represent the total energies of each biphenylene network with and without Li(K) atom, respectively.
E

)
and
E
mono+(n-1)(Li(K)) represent the total energies of each biphenylene network with n and n-1 Li(K) atoms, respectively.
E
Li(K) represents the total energy of a single Li(K) atom in the bulk crystal structure of lithium/potassium. The theoretical specific capacity, depending on the Li(K) atom adsorbed on the networks, is computed using:
C m
=
n x F
M
b i p h e n y l e n e
, where
n
and
x
are the highest possible quantity of adsorbed atoms and electronic charge, respectively. F is Faraday constant, and
M
b i p h e n y l e n e
is the molar mass of biphenylene. The open circuit voltage (OCV) is obtained by the expression [35]:
O C V ≃ −
E
L i
( K )
− b i p h e n y l e n e
−

−

)

)
, where
E
L i
( K )
− b i p h e n y l e n e
,
E
b i p h e n y l e n e
are the total energies of Li(K) atom adsorbed on the network biphenylene networks, pristine biphenylene.
E
L i
( K )
is the energy of a single Li(K) atom in the bulk lattices, e is the charge of Li(K), and
x 2
−
x 1
is the concentration of the adsorbed Li(K) atom, with
x
2
>
x 1
.

Based on the evolutionary algorithm [26], the crystal structure prediction method is used to confirm the thermodynamic stability of all biphenylene networks. An initial population of 40 random structures containing 6 to 12 atoms in each primitive unit cell constitutes the inaugural generation. Subsequent structures are generated through mutation operations on the optimal structures, including 50% heredity, 30% random, 10% lattice mutation, and 10% transmutation ratio. This sequence of evaluating a generation, implementing mutations, and generating a novel set of structures is reiterated for 30 generations in each search session. The algorithmic sampling and screening identify the lowest enthalpy structures maintaining the biphenylene structure. The convex hull diagram is used to investigate the thermodynamic stability. The decomposition energy (
Δ
E d
) is estimated using
Δ
E d
= E
f
–
H
f, where E
f is the formation energy of the predicted structure, and H
f is energy on the convex hull diagram corresponding to the stoichiometry of the CBN structures with respect to previously experimentally synthesized g-C3N3 [36]. The convex hull analysis and corresponding structure are given Fig. 1 and, respectively. Table S1 shows that all predicted biphenylene structures are thermodynamically stable because the decomposition energy satisfies the condition
Δ
E d
≤ 0.

The C6 biphenylene network has 6 carbon atoms in the orthorhombic unit cell with space group Pmmm and optimized lattice constants (a ​= ​4.52 ​Å and b ​= ​3.76 ​Å), consistent with a previous report [37]. The C2B2N2 and B4N4 have a monoclinic crystal structure, while C4BN has a triclinic crystal structure, as shown in Fig. S1. The optimized lattice constants of C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4 biphenylene networks are a ​= ​3.81 ​Å and b ​= ​4.54 ​Å, a ​= ​3.88 ​Å and b ​= ​4.52 ​Å, and a ​= ​5.61 ​Å and b ​= ​4.50 ​Å, respectively. The calculated formation enthalpies of the biphenylene networks, along with graphene and h-BN, are given in Table S1. The formation enthalpy of the B4N4 biphenylene network, C6 biphenylene, h-BN, and graphene are −8.13 ​eV/atom, −8.76 ​eV/atom −8.80 ​eV/atom, and −9.22 ​eV/atom, respectively. Our calculated formation enthalpy values suggest the possible experimental synthesis of these novel biphenylene networks. To further confirm the local minimum energy of our predicted structures, we have also calculated the enthalpy formation energy (See Table S2) of C4BN, C2B2N2, and B3N3 with fixed unit cells having Pmmm space group (as shown in Fig. S2). The calculated enthalpy formation energy is higher compared to our predicted crystal structure.

The calculated phonon band structure of the predicted biphenylene networks is shown in Fig. 2 , which confirms the dynamical stability of our predicted crystal structures. The maximum optical phonon frequency of 49.57 ​THz, 47.40 ​THz, 49.36 ​THz, and 46.08 ​THz for the C6, C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4 biphenylene networks, respectively, is obtained, which is comparable to that of graphene (48.00 ​THz) [38], C6BN (48.44 ​THz) [17] and C3N (47.69 ​THz) [39]. The AIMD simulations confirm the thermal stability of all predicted networks at 1000 ​K, as illustrated in Fig. S3. The Mulliken and Löwdin charge distribution analysis, as implemented in the LOBSTER code [40,41], shows that the charges on the C atoms are redistributed with doped B and N atoms (See Table S3). The calculated Young's modulus, (
Y =
(
C 11 2
−
C 12 2
)
/
C 11
), where C
11 and C
12 are elastic constants, of C6, C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4 are 269.66 Nm−1, 243.13 Nm−1, 222.14 Nm−1, and 278.46 Nm−1, respectively. The bond stiffness decreases with increasing BN concentration, implying that the elastic properties (Table S4) of C6 biphenylene can be tuned by BN doping. The mechanical stability (C11 ​> ​|C12|, C66 ​> ​0) analysis suggests they are mechanically robust. Additionally, the calculated Poisson's ratio of C6, C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4 (0.37, 0.41, 0.42, and 0.35, respectively), shows that the BN biphenylene network has the most mechanically stable structure. The projected Crystal Orbital Hamiltonian Populations (COHP) (Fig. S4) show that the antibonding states decrease with increasing BN doping, implying strong bonding between atoms. The calculated averaged integrated COHP value is −4.84, −3.55, −4.07, and −4.21 ​eV for C6, C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4, respectively.

The electronic band structure of C6, C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4 biphenylene networks is calculated using HSE06 (Fig. 3 ) and PBE (Fig. S5) functionals. The spin-polarized calculations show that the nonmagnetic ground state is more favorable for all biphenylene networks. Our results confirm the metallic behavior of the C6 biphenylene network, which agrees with the previous report [37]. The C4BN also shows metallic behavior with a smaller number of C-2p states crossing the Fermi level. The calculated HSE06 indirect band gap of the C2B2N2 and B4N4 network is 1.29 ​eV and 3.06 ​eV, respectively. The calculated partial density of states (Fig. S6) of C2B2N2 and B4N4 biphenylene networks show that the valence band maxima mostly consist of the N-2p states, whereas the conduction band minima primarily comprise the B-2p states. When BN doping increases (from C6 to B4N4), the contribution from C-2p states decreases, while the contribution from B-2p and N-2p states becomes more prominent in the vicinity of the Fermi level. The valence and conduction band edges belong to different atoms, which has the advantage of separating the photogenerated carriers and extending the lifetime of the exciton, which supports photocatalytic applications [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and diffusion barrier of Li and K ions on the biphenylene networks
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of Li(K) atom on biphenylene networks is investigated using a 3 ​× ​3 ​× ​1 supercell. We tested all possible adsorption sites by calculating the corresponding adsorption energies, in Table S5. Fig. 4 b shows that both Li(K) atom has the highest adsorption energy at the hollow sites of the 8-ring (H8) of C6 and 6-ring (H6) of C2B2N2, indicating the most favorable adsorption sites for Li(K) adsorption. In contrast, the most favorable adsorption site for the Li(K) atom in the case of C4BN is the H6/H8 site. Our results show that the C6 has the best Li(K) adsorption energy of −0.61 ​eV (−0.88 ​eV), followed by C4BN with −0.56 ​eV(-0.81eV), C2B2N2 with −0.14 ​eV (−0.28 ​eV) and B4N4 with 0.33 ​eV (0.19 ​eV) (See Fig. 4b) at their most favorable site. The B4N4 network is not considered in further investigations for battery applications because of the positive adsorption energy of the Li(K) atom. The C atoms have a higher affinity for Li(K) compared to B and N atoms and can be explained by the amount of charge transfer (Bader charge: 0.89 e (0.88 e), 0.84 e (0.85 e), and 0.83 e (0.85 e) to C6, C4BN and C2B2N2, respectively, to the biphenylene networks. The calculated charge density of Li(K) atom adsorb networks is shown in Fig. S7, which also confirms this trend of charge transfer. The COHP calculations are used to investigate the strength of the chemical bond in the biphenylene networks after the Li(K) atom adsorption. Our results show that the structures maintain stability even after redistributing the bonding and antibonding states, as shown in Fig. S8.

The diffusion barrier of Li(K) atom on C6 and C4BN networks is calculated through different paths corresponding to two different configurations (Configuration1: Path 1: H8→ H8, Path 2: H8 → H4 → H8, Path 3: H8 → H6 → H8, Configuration2: Path 4: H6 → H6, Path 5: H6 → H4 → H6, Path 6: H6 → H8 → H6), as shown in Fig. 5 . The Li diffusion on C4BN and C2B2N2 and K diffusion on C2B2N2 possess distinct paths: Path 1: H6 → H6, Path 2: H6 → H4 → H6, Path 3: H6 → H8 → H6, as shown in Figs. 5b and c. The distinct paths in different networks are due to the different adsorption sites of the Li(K) atoms. Interestingly, Li and K have equal valence electrons but migrate through different paths on C6, which could be due to the different ionic sizes (Li-0.76 ​Å and K-1.38 ​Å) [43]. The fastest K mobility paths on the C6, C4BN, and C2B2N2 networks have a diffusion barrier of 66 ​meV (Path 2), 58 ​meV (Path 2), and 79 ​meV (Path 3), respectively. Path 1 is the fastest diffusion of Li on C6 (142 ​meV) and C4BN (178 ​meV), while it is fastest via path 3 of C2B2N2 (251 ​meV). These materials possess enhanced charge/discharge ability compared to 2D materials [1,16]. The diffusion coefficient of Li(K) atom is calculated using the transition-state theory equation (
D =
l 2
×
v 0
exp ( −
E
d
/
k
B
T )
), where l is the hopping distance,
v 0
exp ( −
E
d
/
k
B
T )
is the hopping rate,
v 0
is the vibrational frequency, E
d is the diffusion barrier, k
B is the Boltzmann constant, and T is 300 ​K. The calculated diffusion constant of Li(K) for C6, C4BN, and C2B2N2 is 3.56 ​× ​10−4
cm 2
/ s
(6.70 ​× ​10−3
cm 2
/ s
), 8.50 ​× ​10−5
cm 2
/ s
(8.78 ​× ​10−3
cm 2
/ s
), and 5.33 ​× ​10−6
cm 2
/ s
(4.12 ​× ​10−3
cm 2
/ s
), respectively. The calculated diffusion constants are significantly higher than those on bilayer graphene [44]
10
− 7
cm 2
/ s
and blue phosphorene [45] 8.58
×
10
− 5
cm 2
/ s
.

We have investigated the diffusion barrier of Li(K) atom through optimized bilayer biphenylene networks (Fig. S10) to demonstrate the performance of bilayer biphenylene network as an anode material. The optimized lattice constants of the bilayer biphenylene network are given in Table S7. The calculated intercalation energy of Li(K) in the bilayer C6, C4BN, and C2B2N2 is −1.22 ​eV (−0.75 ​eV), −1.13 ​eV (−0.50 ​eV), and −1.02 ​eV (−0.45 ​eV), respectively, as given in Table S8. Interestingly, the diffusion barrier of K atom through C6 (79 ​meV) and C4BN (110 ​meV) bilayers is lower than that of graphite (180 ​meV) [46]. This is also the case for Li (C6 and C4BN: 223 ​meV and C2B2N2: 172 ​meV, graphite (420 ​meV) [47]).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical storage capacity and open circuit voltage (OCV)
ce:section.ce:para:
Our calculated Li(K) theoretical storage capacity of 950.64 ​mA ​h ​g−1 (826.42 ​mA ​h ​g−1), 940.21 ​mA ​h ​g−1 (899.01 ​mA ​h ​g−1), and 768.08 ​mA ​h ​g−1 (808.47 ​mA ​h ​g−1) for C6, C4BN, and C2B2N2 biphenylene networks, respectively, is shown in Fig. 6 . Our results demonstrate that these biphenylene networks have the potential to be an excellent anode material for commercial batteries because of higher theoretical storage capacity than graphite (372 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [21], holey graphyne (873 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [48] and comparable with monolayer silicene (954 ​mA ​h ​g−1) [49]. The voltage profiles in Figs. 6b to d show all positive values throughout the Li(K) insertion process, which indicates a lower chance of forming dendrites. The Li(K) average OCV of C6, C4BN, and C2B2N2 is 0.23 ​V (0.23 ​V), 0.34 ​V (0.23 ​V), and 0.17 ​V (0.13 ​V), respectively. The energy density is estimated using the relation:
D E
=
|
p o t e n t i a l
v s .
S H E + O C V
|
×
C m
, where the standard hydrogen electrode potential (SHE) of Li(K) is −3.04 ​V(-2.93 ​V) [50], OCV is the average voltage, and C m is the calculated theoretical capacity. The calculated Li(K) energy density of biphenylene networks is 2547.72 ​mW ​h ​g−1(2181.75 ​mW ​h ​g−1) for C6, 2576.18 ​mW ​h ​g−1(2445.31 ​mW ​h ​g−1) for C4BN and 2181.35 ​mW ​h ​g−1(2263.72 ​mW ​h ​g−1) for C2B2N2. These values are significantly higher than other 2D carbon-based anode materials, for example, 822 ​mW ​h ​g−1 for C2N [51] and 1531.81 ​mW ​h ​g−1 for C6BN [17].

Substantial changes in the volume of an electrode during the charging and discharging process can reduce the capacity of a battery, degrade an electrolyte, and reduce its overall efficiency [52,53]. Our results show only a slight volume change of 1.3% (1.6%) in C6, 0.70% (0.93%) in C4BN, and −0.58% (−0.90%) in C2B2N2 after Li(K) adsorption, as shown in Fig. S9. The room temperature thermal stability of the lithiated/potassiated structures is also calculated to investigate the capacity decay rate at elevated temperatures [54] and shown in Fig. S11. The C6 biphenylene is shrunk the most after lithiation/potassiation, while BN doping provides strength to the biphenylene networks.

Using the evolutionary crystal search method within first-principles calculations, we have predicted novel C4BN, C2B2N2, and B4N4 biphenylene networks. Our calculations confirm the dynamic, thermal, and mechanical stability of biphenylene networks. The BN doping in biphenylene networks shows a transition metal to semimetal and semiconductor. The Li(K) adsorption energy on C6, C4BN, and C2B2N2 networks are −0.61 ​eV (−0.88 ​eV), −0.56 ​eV (−0.81 ​eV), and −0.14 ​eV (−0.28 ​eV), respectively. We have obtained a high Li(K) theoretical storage capacity of 950.64 ​mA ​h ​g−1(826.42 ​mA ​h ​g−1) for C6, 940.21 ​mA ​h ​g−1/899.01 ​mA ​h ​g−1 for C4BN, and 768.08 ​mA ​h ​g−1(808.47 ​mA ​h ​g−1) for C2B2N2. Our results show ultrafast Li(K) ion transport with a low diffusion barrier of 142 ​meV(66 ​meV), 178 ​meV(58 ​meV), and 251 ​meV(79 ​meV), respectively, which implies excellent charge/discharge rate of Li(K). The calculated Li(K) energy density of biphenylene networks is 2547.72 ​mW ​h ​g−1(2181.75 ​mW ​h ​g−1) for C6, 2576.18 ​mW ​h ​g−1(2445.31 ​mW ​h ​g−1) for C4BN and 2181.35 ​mW ​h ​g−1(2263.72 ​mW ​h ​g−1) for C2B2N2. Overall, this idea, which sets a paradigm in materials’ prediction, creates the possibility of quick expansion of the 2D materials database and engineering and improving the properties of existing materials. Our findings demonstrate that the C6, C4BN, and C2B2N2 biphenylene networks possess significant and advantageous characteristics, such as high storage capacities, fast Li(K) mobility, low OCV, and high energy density that make them promising anode materials for Li(K) ion batteries. We recommend further experimental investigations to examine the effects of BN doping on the electronic properties of biphenylene networks and applications in various types of batteries.

Adewale Hammed Pasanaje: Investigation, Data curation, Writing original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Revision. Nirpendra Singh: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding, Writing, Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Authors acknowledge the Khalifa University of Science and Technology through the internal grant RIG-2023-01. The authors would like to acknowledge the computational resources and support from the high-performance computing facility Almesbar at Khalifa University of Science and Technology. A. H. P. acknowledges Dr. Daniel Choi for his valuable suggestions.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoms.2024.02.008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of two-dimensional materials as anodes for rechargeable metal-ion batteries: a comprehensive perspective from density functional theory simulations
